solution: 
```cpp
#include <unordered_map>
#include <map>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

class TweetCounts {
public:
    TweetCounts() {
        
    }
    
    void recordTweet(string tweetName, int time) {
        tweets[tweetName].insert(time);
    }
    
    vector<int> getTweetCountsPerFrequency(string freq, string tweetName, int startTime, int endTime) {
        vector<int> result;
        if (!tweets.count(tweetName)) return result;

        int chunkSize;
        if (freq == "minute") chunkSize = 60;
        else if (freq == "hour") chunkSize = 3600;
        else chunkSize = 86400;

        int totalChunks = (endTime - startTime) / chunkSize + 1;

        result.resize(totalChunks, 0);

        for (auto time : tweets[tweetName]) {
            if (time < startTime || time > endTime) continue;
            int chunkIndex = (time - startTime) / chunkSize;
            result[chunkIndex]++;
        }

        return result;
    }

private:
    std::unordered_map<std::string, std::multiset<int>> tweets;
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N + K log K), where N is the number of tweets for the given tweetName and K is the number of chunks
space complexity: O(T), where T is the total number of tweets stored
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a multiset to store tweet times for each tweet name, allowing efficient insertion and lookup. For getTweetCountsPerFrequency, it calculates the number of chunks based on the frequency and iterates through the tweet times to count how many fall into each chunk. The use of a multiset ensures ordered storage and efficient range queries. Key strengths include efficient time complexity and minimal energy usage due to optimized data structures.
